summary: ApoptoSENS is a CAR-NK cell therapy targeting senescent cells via novel surface markers (SENS-1, SENS-2, SENS-3). The project has completed a study and published results, representing a discovery-stage therapeutic program from SENS Research Foundation focused on eliminating senescent cells to reduce age-related inflammation and tissue dysfunction.

content_blocks.title: Project Title and Status
content_blocks.content: ApoptoSENS - Study finished, paper published. This status indicates completion of initial research phase with peer-reviewed publication.

content_blocks.title: Therapeutic Concept
content_blocks.content: Development of Chimeric Antigen Receptor Natural Killer (CAR-NK) cells engineered to selectively recognize and eliminate senescent cells. Approach involves: (1) identification of novel senescence-specific surface markers, (2) validation across models, (3) generation of monoclonal antibodies, (4) construction of CAR-NK cells with improved precision and safety over existing senolytics. Targets accumulation of senescent cells that drive chronic inflammation, tissue dysfunction, and aging-related disease.

content_blocks.title: Target Indication
content_blocks.content: Accumulation of senescent cells - broad aging-related indication rather than specific disease.

content_blocks.title: Mechanism of Action
content_blocks.content: CAR-NK cells engineered to recognize senescence-specific surface antigens (SENS-1, SENS-2, SENS-3) that are absent on healthy cells. Upon target binding, NK cells induce apoptosis in senescent cells, reducing SASP (senescence-associated secretory phenotype)-driven inflammation and tissue dysfunction.

content_blocks.title: Biological Target
content_blocks.content: Novel senescence-associated surface markers discovered in IMR-90 cells (human fibroblasts) rendered senescent by two methods: ionizing radiation or doxorubicin treatment. Three top candidates identified: SENS-1, SENS-2, SENS-3. SENS-3 shows strongest early promise. Cell model: IMR-90 fibroblasts. Senescence induction methods: ionizing radiation, doxorubicin (chemotherapy agent).

content_blocks.title: Current Development Stage
content_blocks.content: Study finished, paper published. This indicates completion of discovery/validation phase with peer-reviewed results, but specific experimental details (computational modeling, in vitro validation, in vivo studies, candidate selection stage) are not detailed in this document.

content_blocks.title: Development Timeline and Milestones
content_blocks.content: Study finished, paper published. No future milestones specified, suggesting project at transition point post-publication.

content_blocks.title: Team Information
content_blocks.content: Key Personnel: Dr. Amit Sharma (Project Lead, SENS Research Foundation). Affiliations: SENS Research Foundation, Senescence Immunology Research Group. Dr. Sharma background: PhD from University of Pune, prior postdoctoral research at Buck Institute. Team Expertise: (1) Cellular senescence biology, (2) Immunology and immune aging, (3) NK-cell and CAR-cell engineering, (4) Target discovery and antibody generation, (5) Translational aging-related therapeutics.

content_blocks.title: Intellectual Property Status
content_blocks.content: Patent Application Status: Not specified. Scope of IP Protection: Not specified (no information on composition of matter, method of use, or target claims). Freedom-to-Operate Assessment: Not specified (no information on blocking patents or FTO concerns provided).

key_findings: Project status: Study completed with paper published, indicating completion of discovery phase research
Therapeutic modality: CAR-NK cells targeting senescent cells - novel approach compared to small molecule senolytics
Target discovery: Three novel senescence-specific surface markers identified (SENS-1, SENS-2, SENS-3) with SENS-3 showing strongest promise
Model system: IMR-90 human fibroblasts with senescence induced by ionizing radiation or doxorubicin
Team has relevant expertise in senescence biology, immunology, CAR-cell engineering, and antibody generation
IP status unclear - no information provided on patents, claims scope, or freedom-to-operate
No quantitative experimental data provided in this overview document (efficacy, selectivity, potency data)
Development stage appears to be TRL 2-3: target discovery completed, validation in cell models done (publication), but no information on disease-relevant models or in vivo studies
No future milestones or regulatory pathway specified, suggesting early/discovery stage program
